name: Ioan Sârca
wiki_summary: Ioan Sârca (9 March 1911 – 1991), known as The Monster from Valcău, was a Romanian serial killer and rapist who killed at least 20 young boys and adolescents between 1943 and 1945. An illiterate factory worker, he lured victims with promises of employment, then strangled them and sold their clothes at market stalls. He escaped prison using forged Soviet citizenship papers and resumed killing before being recaptured in Oradea. Sentenced to two life terms, he died in prison in 1991 without remorse.

name: Hugo Schenk
wiki_summary: Hugo Schenk was an Austrian serial killer and swindler who killed four housemaids in Vienna in 1883. He posed as a Polish aristocrat to seduce women, then murdered them and disposed of their bodies in the Danube. He was caught when a body washed ashore and was executed by hanging in 1884.

name: Peter Niers
alias: The Sorcerer of the Black Forest
wiki_summary: Peter Niers was a German serial killer and bandit active in the late 16th century who confessed under torture to 544 murders across the Holy Roman Empire. He led a gang disguised as shepherds and was believed to practice black magic using body parts of his victims. His three-day execution in 1581 was among the most gruesome in recorded history.

name: Karl Denke
wiki_summary: Karl Denke was a German serial killer and cannibal known as Papa Denke, beloved in the community of Münsterberg as a generous church-going landlord. After his arrest in 1924, police discovered he had murdered at least 30 vagrants over two decades, pickling their flesh in brine and selling it as pork at local markets. He hanged himself in his cell before trial.

name: Pierre Basson
wiki_summary: Pierre Basson is considered South Africa's earliest documented serial killer, murdering nine people between 1903 and 1906 in the Cape Colony. He killed farmers and travelers across isolated rural areas, evading capture for years. When police finally closed in, he shot himself rather than be arrested.

name: Locusta of Gaul
wiki_summary: Locusta was a professional poisoner in the Roman Empire who worked at the behest of Emperor Nero and his mother Agrippina. She poisoned Emperor Claudius and Britannicus, Nero's rival. Nero rewarded her with estates and pupils to teach her craft. She was executed under Emperor Galba after Nero's death and is considered one of history's first documented contract killers.

name: Peter Stumpp
wiki_summary: Peter Stumpp was a German farmer executed in 1589 for murders, cannibalism, and incest committed over 25 years near Bedburg. Under torture he confessed to killing 14 children and two pregnant women and eating the brains of his own son. His case became one of the most famous werewolf trials in history and directly shaped werewolf folklore across central Europe.

name: Cayetano Santos Godino
wiki_summary: Cayetano Santos Godino, known as Petiso Orejudo (Big-Eared Midget), was an Argentine serial killer who began murdering young children in Buenos Aires at age seven. He killed four children and attempted many more murders before his arrest at sixteen in 1912. His case shocked Argentina and prompted national debate about juvenile criminality. He died in prison in 1944.

name: Mariam Soulakiotis
wiki_summary: Mariam Soulakiotis was a Greek abbess who allegedly killed up to 177 people at her convent in Keratea near Athens, including wealthy women who had donated their fortunes to the sect. Victims died through starvation, enforced fasting, and abuse. She was arrested in 1951, convicted, and died in prison in 1957 — one of the most unusual cases in 20th century European criminal history.

name: Péter Kovács
wiki_summary: Péter Kovács, the Monster of Martfű, was a Hungarian serial killer who strangled five women over ten years in the small industrial town of Martfű. Communist authorities suppressed the investigation, refusing to acknowledge that serial killers could exist in a socialist society. An innocent man served years in prison for his crimes before Kovács was caught in 1967 and executed.

name: Roger Andermatt
wiki_summary: Roger Andermatt was a Swiss nurse and the most prolific serial killer in Swiss history, convicted of murdering 22 elderly patients in nursing homes around Lucerne. He injected victims with tranquilizers before smothering them, claiming compassion for their suffering. By night, he lived a parallel life as a DJ under the name R.O.-Gee. He was sentenced to life imprisonment in 2005.

name: Saeed Hanaei
wiki_summary: Saeed Hanaei was an Iranian construction worker who murdered 16 sex workers in Mashhad between 2000 and 2001, believing he was cleansing the city of moral corruption. His case divided Iranian society, with some publicly celebrating him as a vigilante hero. He was executed in 2002. A documentary about his case was banned in Iran.

name: Thug Behram
wiki_summary: Thug Behram was a leader of the Thuggee cult in India who strangled between 125 and 931 travelers as religious sacrifices to the goddess Kali in the early 19th century. The Thuggees would befriend travelers before killing them, operating across central India for generations. Behram was hanged by British colonial authorities around 1840, and his cult gave the English language the word 'thug'.

name: Liu Pengli
wiki_summary: Liu Pengli, nephew of Han Emperor Jing of China, is one of the earliest documented serial killers in recorded history. As Prince of Jidong in the 2nd century BC, he led raids to murder people for sport, killing over 100 subjects across 29 years of his reign. Due to his royal blood, he was merely stripped of his title and exiled rather than executed — an early example of power protecting a killer.

name: Jack Unterweger
wiki_summary: Jack Unterweger was an Austrian serial killer who became a celebrated author while serving a life sentence for his first murder. His literary rehabilitation convinced authorities to release him in 1990, after which he immediately resumed killing across Austria, Czechoslovakia, and the United States. He hanged himself hours after being convicted in 1994. His case became a parable about the dangers of society's need for redemption narratives.

name: Belle Gunness
wiki_summary: Belle Gunness was a Norwegian-American serial killer who placed marriage advertisements in newspapers to lure suitors to her Indiana farm, where she murdered them for insurance money. She killed between 25 and 40 people including two husbands and her own children. Her farmhouse burned in 1908 — with a headless female body inside that may or may not have been hers. She was never definitively caught.

name: Florencio Fernández
wiki_summary: Florencio Fernández, known as the Argentine Vampire, allegedly killed 15 women in Tucumán Province during the 1950s. His case sits on the border between documented fact and legend — some Argentine historians have questioned the official record while others confirm multiple convictions. He died in prison in 1968, leaving behind a story that is as much about the mythology of crime as the crimes themselves.

name: Caroline Grills
wiki_summary: Caroline Grills, known as Auntie Thally, was an Australian serial poisoner who killed at least five family members and acquaintances using thallium — a tasteless, odorless rat poison — in New South Wales between 1947 and 1953. A cheerful grandmother figure beloved by her community, she was finally caught when relatives noticed her watching them intently after they drank her tea. She died in prison in 1960.

name: Gesche Gottfried
wiki_summary: Gesche Gottfried was a German serial killer known as the Angel of Bremen who poisoned 15 people over 14 years using arsenic-laced mouse butter — including two husbands, her parents, her children, and close friends. She was adored as a devoted caregiver and mourner at funerals of her own victims. Her public execution in 1831 was the last in Bremen, attended by thousands.

name: Snowy Rowles
wiki_summary: Snowy Rowles was an Australian stockman who murdered three men in the remote Murchison region of Western Australia and dissolved their bodies in campfire ash — a technique he had read about in an unpublished detective novel by Arthur Upfield. He was caught only because he forgot one step in the process after the third murder. He was executed in 1932 in one of Australian history's most unusual murder cases.

name: William Burke
wiki_summary: William Burke and William Hare were Irish immigrants in Edinburgh who murdered 16 people in 1827 and 1828 to sell fresh corpses to surgeon Robert Knox for medical dissection. Unlike grave robbers, they created their own supply. Burke was executed in 1829 — and publicly dissected, his skeleton displayed in Edinburgh to this day. His name entered the English language as the verb 'to burke', meaning to suppress.

name: Leonarda Cianciulli
wiki_summary: Leonarda Cianciulli was an Italian serial killer known as the Soap-Maker of Correggio who murdered three women in 1939–1940, believing human sacrifice would protect her son serving in World War II. She rendered the bodies into soap and baked the remaining flesh into teacakes, which she served to neighbors. Her case remains singular in Italian criminal history for its combination of the domestic and the monstrous.

name: Manuel Delgado Villegas
wiki_summary: Manuel Delgado Villegas, known as El Arropiero after the sweet syrup his family sold, was a Spanish serial killer who confessed to 48 murders across Spain between 1964 and 1971. Illiterate and nomadic, he killed hitchhikers and travelers with no apparent pattern. Under Franco's regime he was declared insane and committed to a psychiatric institution. He was released in 1998 and died in 2010.

name: Abul Djabar
wiki_summary: Abul Djabar was an Afghan serial killer who strangled at least 65 men and boys using turbans while committing sexual assaults, with suspicions of over 300 victims. Operating across Afghanistan during the 1960s, he preyed on travelers and young men. He was sentenced to death and hanged in 1970, representing one of the most prolific yet least-documented cases in Asian criminal history.

name: Gilles de Rais
wiki_summary: Gilles de Rais was a French Marshal and companion-in-arms to Joan of Arc who became one of history's earliest documented serial killers. After Joan's execution, he retired to his Breton estates where he abducted, tortured, and murdered hundreds of children over eight years. Convicted by both ecclesiastical and secular courts, he was hanged and burned in 1440 — history's most dramatic fall from chivalric glory.

name: Carl Panzram
wiki_summary: Carl Panzram was an American serial killer who described himself as the living consequence of everything society had done to him — systematically brutalized from childhood in reformatories. He killed across multiple continents and once stole a yacht belonging to former President Taft. He confessed to 21 murders and hundreds of rapes. He thanked his executioner before being hanged in 1930.

name: Yoo Young-chul
wiki_summary: Yoo Young-chul murdered 21 people across Seoul in under a year in 2003–2004, alternating between wealthy elderly victims and sex workers. Released from prison months before his spree began, he expressed deep hatred for both the rich and women. He ate the livers of some of his victims. Sentenced to death in 2005, he remains on death row — South Korea's most chilling modern criminal case.

name: Pedro Rodrigues Filho
wiki_summary: Pedro Rodrigues Filho, known as Pedrinho Matador, committed his first murder at fourteen and killed over 70 people — mostly criminals and corrupt officials. He killed his own father in prison and reportedly ate part of his heart. Sentenced to 128 years but released after 40 under Brazilian law, he became a YouTube personality discussing his crimes, turning true evil into content.

name: Thomas Neill Cream
wiki_summary: Thomas Neill Cream was a Scottish-Canadian physician who poisoned women with strychnine across Canada, the United States, and England between 1879 and 1892. Active in London just after Jack the Ripper, he taunted police with letters implicating innocent men. He was hanged in 1892 and reportedly uttered 'I am Jack the—' before the trapdoor opened, a claim that has never been resolved.

name: Nikolai Dzhumagaliev
wiki_summary: Nikolai Dzhumagaliev, known as Metal Fang for his white metal teeth, was a Kazakh serial killer and cannibal who murdered at least seven women in Soviet Kazakhstan during the 1980s. Found not guilty by reason of insanity, he was committed to a psychiatric hospital from which he escaped in 1989. He was recaptured in Uzbekistan in 1991, allegedly having killed again. One of the Soviet Union's most disturbing unsolved legacies.

name: Moses Sithole
wiki_summary: Moses Sithole, the ABC Killer, raped and strangled 38 women across Atteridgeville, Boksburg, and Cleveland in 1994–1995, luring them with false employment promises. His crimes unfolded in the chaotic early years of post-apartheid South Africa when institutions were in transition. He received a sentence of 2,410 years — one of the longest in legal history — a symbolic accounting that no single life could satisfy.

name: Ahmad Suradji
wiki_summary: Ahmad Suradji, known as Datuk, was an Indonesian cattle breeder and self-proclaimed sorcerer who murdered 42 women and girls near Medan, Sumatra, over eleven years. He claimed his dead father's spirit instructed him to kill 70 women and drink their saliva to gain supernatural power. He buried victims upright to their waists before strangling them. He was executed by firing squad in 2008.

name: Miyuki Ishikawa
wiki_summary: Miyuki Ishikawa was a Japanese midwife who killed between 85 and 169 newborn infants in postwar Tokyo by leaving them to die of neglect, convincing impoverished parents that death was merciful. Despite the staggering victim count she received only eight years in prison. Her case was so disturbing it directly prompted Japan to legalize abortion in 1948 — one killer inadvertently reshaping an entire nation's law.

name: Javed Iqbal
wiki_summary: Javed Iqbal confessed in a letter to police to murdering exactly 100 boys in Lahore, dissolving their bodies in vats of acid. He surrendered to a newspaper rather than police and demanded to be executed in the same way — cut into 100 pieces and dissolved. He was found dead in his cell before sentencing, officially a suicide, though the circumstances remain disputed.

name: Genene Jones
wiki_summary: Genene Jones was an American pediatric nurse suspected of killing between 11 and 46 infant patients in Texas using lethal injections, then attempting to resuscitate them to appear heroic. Hospital administrators destroyed evidence and allowed her to resign and move to a new facility rather than face scandal — effectively enabling further killings. Her case became a landmark study in institutional complicity.

name: Peter Kürten
wiki_summary: Peter Kürten terrorized Düsseldorf in 1929 with a series of hammer attacks and strangulations on men, women, and children. Known as the Vampire of Düsseldorf, he reportedly drank blood from some victims and was described as extraordinarily ordinary in appearance. Married and well-regarded by neighbors. He was convicted of nine murders and executed by guillotine in 1931, reportedly asking whether he would hear his own blood flowing.

name: Yang Xinhai
wiki_summary: Yang Xinhai was a Chinese drifter who murdered 67 people and raped 23 women across four provinces between 1999 and 2003, making him the most prolific serial killer in modern Chinese history. He attacked sleeping families with axes, hammers, and shovels before vanishing into rural anonymity. When captured he showed no remorse, saying he killed simply because he had nothing to live for. He was executed in 2004.

name: Anatoly Onoprienko
wiki_summary: Anatoly Onoprienko murdered 52 people including nine entire families across Ukraine between 1989 and 1996, leaving no survivors at any scene — killing witnesses including small children. After his arrest he claimed God had given him instructions and felt no remorse. He issued a press release from prison stating his goal was to set a world record. His death sentence was commuted to life; he died in prison in 2013.

name: John Bunting
wiki_summary: John Bunting is considered Australia's worst serial killer, a charismatic man who recruited followers from his working-class social circle to torture and kill 11 people in South Australia. He targeted those he deemed undesirable. The victims' bodies were stored in barrels of acid in a disused bank vault in the tiny town of Snowtown — a discovery that became one of the most disturbing in Australian history.

name: Donald Neilson
wiki_summary: Donald Neilson, the Black Panther, was a former British soldier who committed four methodical murders during post office robberies in northern England, then kidnapped 17-year-old heiress Lesley Whittle and held her suspended underground in a drainage shaft, where she died. His military precision and years of evasion made him a defining figure in British criminal history. He died in prison in 2011.

name: Serhiy Tkach
wiki_summary: Serhiy Tkach was a Ukrainian serial killer and former criminal investigator who used his professional knowledge to evade detection for 25 years while killing over 100 girls and women. An innocent man served years in prison for some of his crimes. He was convicted of 37 murders in 2008 but claimed the total exceeded 100. The case stands as one of the most damning indictments of post-Soviet policing.

name: Joachim Kroll
wiki_summary: Joachim Kroll was a German toilet attendant and cannibal who murdered at least eight — possibly 14 — people across the Ruhr valley over 21 years. Police had questioned him multiple times without suspicion. He was caught in 1976 when neighbors reported a blocked drain; police found a child's hand cooking in his kitchen. Simple-minded and cooperative with interrogators, he seemed genuinely not to understand why anyone was upset.

name: Kuno Hofmann
wiki_summary: Kuno Hofmann was a German serial killer known as the Vampire of Nuremberg who killed three people in 1971–1972 and exhumed and drank blood from corpses in a cemetery. A severe social misfit with profound mental illness, he evaded capture for months. His case reignited vampire mythology in postwar West Germany and became one of the most bizarre criminal cases of the era.

name: Béla Kiss
wiki_summary: Béla Kiss was a Hungarian serial killer who lured at least 24 women through matrimonial newspaper advertisements, insured them, then murdered and pickled their bodies in large metal drums on his estate near Budapest. He disappeared at the outbreak of World War I. The drums were discovered in 1916 by soldiers looking for fuel. Kiss was reportedly spotted multiple times over the following decades but was never found.

name: Pierre Rivière
wiki_summary: Pierre Rivière was a French Norman peasant who in 1835 killed his mother, sister, and brother with a billhook, then fled into the forest and voluntarily surrendered. He had written a remarkable memoir explaining his act in meticulous and intelligent detail, which fascinated the French medical and legal establishment. His case was later examined by Michel Foucault as a study of power, madness, and narrative. He died by suicide in prison in 1840.

name: Wang Qiang
wiki_summary: Wang Qiang murdered 26 people — mostly women and children — across Shenyang between 1995 and 1999, causing widespread public panic in a city of millions. He evaded police for four years by moving between neighborhoods, leaving no consistent pattern. He was executed in 2000. His case exposed critical failures in China's capacity to investigate serial crime across dense urban environments.

name: Pedro Alonso López
wiki_summary: Pedro Alonso López confessed to raping and murdering over 300 young girls across Colombia, Peru, and Ecuador — among the highest victim counts in documented history. Convicted of 110 murders in Ecuador, he was sentenced to just 16 years and released in 1998 on good behavior. He was briefly detained in Colombia in 2002 but released. His current whereabouts are unknown. He may still be alive.

name: Ion Rîmaru
wiki_summary: Ion Rîmaru was a Romanian medical student who terrorized Bucharest in 1970–1971, committing four murders and multiple rapes. He attacked women at night and reportedly drank blood from victims, earning the name Vampire of Bucharest. His father Florea Rîmaru had also been a serial killer two decades earlier — one of the only known father-and-son serial killer pairs in history. Ion was executed in 1971.

name: Javed Iqbal Mughal
wiki_summary: Javed Iqbal Mughal was a Pakistani serial killer who dissolved the bodies of 100 young boys in vats of acid in Lahore, then sent a detailed confession to police and a newspaper demanding to be executed the same way. His controlled, theatrical self-surrender — bypassing police in favor of the press — created a media event that shocked Pakistan and raised questions about justice that were never resolved when he died in his cell.

name: Fritz Haarmann
wiki_summary: Fritz Haarmann murdered at least 24 young men in Hannover between 1918 and 1924, during the grinding poverty of the Weimar Republic. A police informant, he preyed on homeless boys at the railway station, killing by biting through their throats. He sold their possessions and allegedly their flesh as black market meat during a famine period. He was decapitated in 1925, reportedly requesting it be done swiftly so the public could see his face.
